description: Pinned perf baseline for the PFSF CPU microbench. scripts/pfsf_perf_gate.py compares build/pfsf-bench/results.json against these budgets. Every primitive has a Java-ref upper bound (java_ns_per_op_max) and a lower bound on the native speedup when the .so is loaded (native_over_java_min). Leaving native_over_java_min as null disables the native check for that row (used when the .so is unavailable on the current runner, or when a primitive's budget is temporarily decoupled from the speedup check — see tick50k_surrogate below). Regression policy: any java_ns_per_op exceeding its max by more than tolerance_pct fails CI; any native_over_java below its min by more than tolerance_pct also fails.

runner.note: Budgets re-calibrated on the GitHub Actions ubuntu-22.04 runner fleet (2-core @ ~3.25 GHz, 7 GB RAM). Re-pin on runner-hardware changes via scripts/pfsf_perf_gate.py --update-baseline. The -march=x86-64-v3 compile flag (L1-native/CMakeLists.txt) is load-bearing for the native speedup targets below.

primitives.name: normalize_soa6_64k
primitives.note: Bandwidth-bound 64k × 7 floats (source+rcomp+rtens+4·cond). AVX2 auto-vectorisation (-march=x86-64-v3) takes this to ~3× on the runner fleet; 1.8× floor absorbs noise.

primitives.name: chebyshev_table_64
primitives.note: Tiny 64-entry table — JNI boundary cost plus HotSpot JIT warmup noise dominate any native speedup at this scale, so the native check is intentionally disabled. Java-ref max raised to 200 ns to absorb warmup jitter seen on the 2-core runners (observed ~150 ns at cold fork start).

primitives.name: apply_wind_bias_64k
primitives.note: Six direction passes, each a scalar multiply. Java reference auto-vectorises well on HotSpot; native needs -march=x86-64-v3 to match. 1.5× floor is the v0.3e acceptance for this primitive (tighter target pending M3 plan-buffer JNI amortisation).

primitives.name: tick50k_surrogate
primitives.note: The v0.3d delivery gate *in spirit*. 100 reps × (normalize + wind bias + omega table) on 50k voxels. native_over_java is intentionally disabled until v0.3e M3 lands the PFSFTickPlanner plan-buffer batching — today's implementation still issues 3 JNI calls per rep, which makes the ratio dominated by boundary overhead rather than compute. Java-ref max keeps regression detection alive on the aggregate path; flip native_over_java_min back to 1.4 when M3 merges and amortises to ≤3 JNI calls per full tick.
